source.summary: Weekly POC check-in focused on closing staging access gaps and validating quantized model performance. Customer needs SSO/KMS integration and VPC peering for Private deployment trial. Team agreed to run a targeted shadow benchmark and to exchange security artifacts. Some tuning suggested for KV cache sizing and batching policy to hit tail-latency SLOs.
source.transcript:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-09-05 16:30 UTC
Duration: 42m
Attendees: Maya Chen (Redwood AE), Ethan Park (Redwood SE), Aisha Rahman (CTO, NimbleHealth), Tom Belanger (Infra Lead), Sofia Lopez (Product)

Summary: quick sync on onboarding tasks, quantization sprint results, and what needs to be done to get staging green.

[00:00] Maya Chen: ok hey everyone thanks for carving time, um this is the weekly POC sync. we'll run through open access, bench numbers, and then next steps. Ethan, you want to start with the quant results?

[00:28] Ethan Park: yeah sure, so we ran the 7B quantized profile on the shadow runs we had, not full prod scale yet, um average p99 got to about 420ms token on the short responses and tail was spiky on some long prompt sequences, like multi-turn with 2k context. There is also a small quality delta on entity extraction prompts, about 2.3% lower F1 on our labeled set.

[01:03] Aisha Rahman: when you say "quantized profile" is that the int8 profile you recommended last week? I just want to be sure it's the same baseline we looked at in the doc.

[01:15] Ethan Park: yes, it's the int8-mixed pathway — we called it q-int8-v2 in the notes. note: it looks like for several of your longer queries the K V cache hits aren't optimal because the prefix chunking is different from the training shuff — so that increases memory churn and causes transient mid-stream stalls.

[01:45] Tom Belanger: can you unpack that KV thing in plain english? we don't want surprises when we flip to Private. is this a runtime tweak or model change?

[01:58] Ethan Park: mainly runtime — how we slice the prefix for continuous batching interacts with the KV cache size. If you keep small kv shards it thrashes on long contexts. increasing cache allocation or tweaking batch window to prioritize session affinity helps.

[02:25] Sofia Lopez: sorry quick question, if we push more cache does that increase cost significantly? thin client cost concerns from product.

[02:34] Ethan Park: small uptick in GPU memory footprint, cost delta minimal for Dedicated pools, more noticeable if you spin up extra instances purely for memory. But there's a middle ground: smarter batch window + session pinning reduces both tail and memory without doubling nodes.

[03:05] Maya Chen: ok good. on the access side, Tom, last week you were going to create the VPC peering and the bastion host. status?

[03:16] Tom Belanger: I started the peering request, hit a quota limit on our cloud account for cross-region routes, so I'm filing a ticket with infra ops today. I can work around by spinning the staging in region eu-west-1 where you have nodes, but need to confirm IP whitelists and the SAML assertion flow for SSO.

[03:45] Aisha Rahman: the SSO piece is high priority for us — compliance requires SAML for pilot users. also KMS key management is must-have, we can't have keys outside of our HSM.

[03:58] Maya Chen: understood. we'll send the SSO checklist and KMS integration notes after this call. note that our private package supports customer-managed KMS and we have an HSM pattern in the docs. I'll attach pointers.

[04:18] Ethan Park: one subtle thing: when you run private with customer KMS, there is an extra latency hop when retrieving ephemeral certs for certain key derivation steps; that's usually sub-30ms but worth measuring in your bench.

[04:40] Sofia Lopez: is that something we need to simulate in our staging bench or will Redwood include it in the shadow runs?

[04:48] Ethan Park: we can include it if you provide KMS endpoints or a mock in staging. otherwise we'll simulate a baseline added latency.

[05:11] Maya Chen: bench plan — Ethan will run a 1k query shadow benchmark with a mix of short chat prompts and long extraction prompts, we'll capture p50/p95/p99 and quality metrics from the canonical prompt set Sofia will share. Any objections?

[05:25] Aisha Rahman: no objections. please include error rates and memory utilization so we can see if cache thrash correlates with errors.

[05:35] Ethan Park: yep will do. also I'll include a run with the 'less-aggressive' quant profile you saw (int8-loose) to show accuracy tradeoffs.

[05:47] Tom Belanger: on network, I'll prioritize the peering ticket and confirm whether we can provision in region. if not, I will provide a secure jump host and policy docs by Thursday.

[06:01] Maya Chen: perfect. security docs — we'll share SOC2 summary, audit log examples, and the SSO/KMS checklist. note: the audit log includes token-level traces which you can feed into your SIEM, link to example: https://redwood.example.com/security/pilot-audit-sample (placeholder).

[06:28] Sofia Lopez: for success metrics, we want to see p99 under 700ms for typical flows and less than 3% QA regression on extraction tasks. if we hit both for two weeks the PM team will greenlight moving to a larger pilot.

[06:46] Ethan Park: that's achievable, the main knobs will be batch window, cache allocation, and whether you run the int8-loose vs int8-tight profile. We'll iterate.

[07:00] Aisha Rahman: also, any vendor lock concerns? one of our platform folks asked about how easy it is to export weights and move to a self-hosted runtime later.

[07:12] Maya Chen: we provide model variants and versioned artifacts; the objective is to keep serving abstraction consistent so you can port. there are differences in ops, but we can provide export notes. I'll add that to the docs pack.

[07:35] Ethan Park: also we touched briefly on fallback policies — if the private pool is saturated we'll route to a compatible hosted variant with downgraded context length. We can show the failover steps in a runbook.

[07:50] Tom Belanger: good. for audit we need to see how rollbacks are tracked in the console. is that in the same security doc?

[07:58] Maya Chen: yes, runbook + rollback audit is part of the packet. we'll include a short video walkthrough.

[08:20] Sofia Lopez: timeline check — our demo is on 2026-09-14 internal review. do we have enough time to run bench, tune, and prepare demo script?

[08:32] Maya Chen: it's tight but possible. Ethan to run bench by 9/10, results 9/11, then 9/12 tune pass, 9/13 dry-run with PMs, 9/14 demo. We all agreed.

[09:00] Aisha Rahman: can we also get an itemized cost delta for lower-latency config vs baseline? finance wants a quick estimate.

[09:12] Ethan Park: I'll produce a simple per-10k-token delta table for the quant profiles and for increased cache sizing. won't be perfect but should be directional.

[09:28] Maya Chen: ok last bits — any blockers we haven't captured?

[09:33] Tom Belanger: quota ticket is the current blocker; otherwise SSO is paperwork.

[09:40] Sofia Lopez: none from product, just waiting on prompt set finalization which I'll send after this call.

[09:46] Maya Chen: great. recap of next steps and owners — Ethan: bench and cost delta; Maya: security docs and runbook; Tom: peering/bastion; Sofia: prompt set and demo script. We'll sync asynchronously and reconvene same time next week unless we need an ad-hoc deep dive.

[10:05] Aisha Rahman: thanks all.

[10:08] Maya Chen: thanks everyone, bye.

End transcript.

Notes on transcription: minor mis-hearings in model names (q-int8-v2 sometimes transcribed as q-int-8 v two), occasional speaker label ambiguity at 01:03 (speaker labeled as Ethan but might have been Maya).

Main concerns: tail-latency spikes during burst tests, KV cache warm-up time, and SSO/KMS validation. source.transcript:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-06-10
Time: 16:00 UTC
Duration: ~49 minutes
Recording: yes
Links shared: https://redwood.app/pilot/nimblehealth/sprint5-perf, runbook: https://redwood.app/docs/pilot-runbook.pdf

AUTO SUMMARY (auto-generated, may be noisy):
- Sprint 5 perf improved 18% on avg token cost, but tail latency at 99th still shows spikes when cold KV cache is hit.
- Agreed on A/B canary plan (5% -> 25% increments) gated by latency and error budget.
- Next steps: pricing, batching config, soak test, go/no-go on 2026-06-18.

Topics:
- Results review and anomalies
- Canary and AB planning
- Monitoring and runbook handoffs
- Security questions (KMS/VPC)

Action items recap at end.

Transcript body:
00:00:01 Ava Chen: All right everyone, thanks for joining, appreciate the time. Quick roll call?
00:00:08 Dana Ortiz: Hi, Dana here from NimbleHealth, CTO.
00:00:11 Benji Park: Benji, platform, hey.
00:00:13 Leah Solomon: Leah, SRE.
00:00:15 Omar Ruiz: Omar, product.
00:00:17 Marcus Lee: Marcus from Redwood, SE.
00:00:19 Ava Chen: Ava, AE. Priya's on but she might pop in and out.
00:00:25 Priya Nair: Hey team, sorry for being late, I'm here.

00:01:05 Ava Chen: OK quick agenda: results review, major incidents since last week, tuning items Marcus will cover, then finalizing canary / A-B and the decision gates. Should be about 45 minutes.
00:01:20 Dana Ortiz: Sounds good. High level we've been running the daytime soak and then spikes overnight when batch jobs run. We saw a couple of 5xxs in the logstream.
00:01:33 Marcus Lee: Right, thanks. I'll share the tuned config — note that the batching window we pushed from 20ms to 40ms reduced average token cost by 12% but introduced a 5-10ms bump on p95 when request concurrency is low.
00:01:50 Benji Park: Can you repeat the numbers, you said 12% on cost but what about p99?
00:01:56 Marcus Lee: p99 we still see spikes — roughly 220ms in the worst bursts; a lot of that is KV cache cold misses on long-tail queries. We're experimenting with pre-warm hooks to populate the KV cache for the common prefixes.
00:02:15 Leah Solomon: The cold miss pattern matches our replay — when synthetic traffic ramps we get a warmup over ~18 minutes which is painful for SLAs.
00:02:24 Ava Chen: Is there any opportunity to change model sequence length defaults or prompt engineering to reduce cache churn?
00:02:31 Omar Ruiz: We did try trimming system prompts but it changed quality a bit, users noticed. We prefer application-side prefix caching rather than removing context.
00:02:43 Marcus Lee: Ok reasonable. One approach is a hybrid: keep the context but use prefix hashing to make cache buckets more efficient, reduces blob size. We'll post config.

00:05:00 Ava Chen: Marcus, show the chart for this week's traffic and the 99th percentile.
00:05:10 Marcus Lee: (screen-share) here's day by day, green is avg latency, orange is p95, red is p99. You can see the overnight job at ~03:00 UTC produces the tall red spike; that's correlating with a BI job hitting large context requests.
00:05:38 Benji Park: That BI job unfortunately is pinned to our data warehouse schedule — can't move it easily. We might need to throttle or queue the requests that hit the inference plane.
00:05:48 Leah Solomon: We can add an ingress throttle rule for heavy jobs, and Redwood's rate-limiter hooks will help.
00:05:56 Marcus Lee: Speaking of rate-limiter, we've added a burst queue that defers to a background processing lane. It'll preserve SLA for interactive traffic while smoothing batch loads.

00:08:12 Dana Ortiz: On security: last week you mentioned KMS integration for Private — status? We need HSM-backed keys and audit logs for compliance.
00:08:22 Marcus Lee: Yes, our Private plane supports customer-managed keys via standard KMS and HSM integrations. At the moment we have a KMS endpoint test working, but we need Nimble to confirm key policy and network egress for the KMS calls.
00:08:38 Benji Park: Is the KMS call origin from the control plane or the inference nodes? We need to know to allow egress from our network egress rules.
00:08:45 Marcus Lee: It's from the inference nodes for key unwrap, with optional cache TTL on the node. The control plane does not access raw keys. We'll document exact FQDNs and IP ranges.
00:08:56 Dana Ortiz: OK please send that doc — we have to clear it with security by 6/16.

00:10:30 Ava Chen: Back to rollout: we propose an A/B routing approach for the pilot cutover. Start with baseline on current in-house model for 95% and Redwood on 5% for the first day, then expand 5 -> 25 -> 50 based on gates.
00:10:45 Omar Ruiz: Why not start with 10%? 5% might be too small to get meaningful equity metrics for product signals.
00:10:52 Marcus Lee: 5% is conservative to prove stability. If you want we can do 10% as long as the gating thresholds are stricter for p99 and error rate.
00:11:05 Leah Solomon: We care about three gates: p99 latency under 350ms, error rate under 0.2%, and token-cost delta within the allocated budget. If any gate trips we rollback automatically.
00:11:20 Benji Park: What's the rollback latency? We need it to be under 30s to avoid user-impacting sessions.
00:11:25 Marcus Lee: With our routing fabric, rollback is immediate at the edge; you can expect traffic shift under ~10 seconds if the health signals are triggered.
00:11:33 Ava Chen: We'll include an automated canary monitor in the Redwood console and a slack alert for any deviation.

00:13:00 Dana Ortiz: On the canary metrics — can Redwood emit token-level cost per route and per-token latency so we can reconcile billing?
00:13:08 Marcus Lee: Yes, we have per-route cost breakdown and token latency histograms. We'll share a sample dashboard schema.
00:13:15 Ava Chen: I'll attach the sample cost dashboard (links in notes).

00:14:40 Omar Ruiz: One open question — how do we handle fallbacks for degraded outputs? E.g., if model hallucination spikes, do we fallback to a smaller deterministic reranker?
00:14:51 Marcus Lee: Good question. We recommend a fallback policy: if quality regressions detected by eval sets (we'll attach prompt tests), route to a conservative model variant or to the legacy pipeline. The Redwood console supports function-based hooks to check similarity scores and apply routing rules.
00:15:12 Leah Solomon: We need that to be automatic — humans can't watch 24/7.
00:15:16 Ava Chen: We'll push a policy template for quality-based fallback with example thresholds.

00:18:00 Marcus Lee: Quick demo of the tuned batching config — note this is the one we want you to run in the soak. It includes a pre-warm hook for top-200 prefixes and a KV cache TTL bump to 45m in the early window.
00:18:18 Benji Park: If TTL is 45m does that create memory pressure on the node?
00:18:22 Marcus Lee: Slightly, but we've tuned eviction to LRU tiers so hot prefixes persist while cold ones get evicted. We'll provide recommended node sizing.
00:18:30 Benji Park: Please put sizing recommendations in the doc.

00:22:45 Dana Ortiz: On observability, can we get trace sampling of 100% during the canary window? We want full traces for the first 24 hours.
00:22:54 Marcus Lee: Yes, sampling can be set per-route in the console. For the canary we'll set 100% traces and forward to your tracing endpoint — there's a config for redaction too for PII.
00:23:05 Leah Solomon: And alerting — we'll want a runbook with exact paging rules. Who owns alerts during the cutover?
00:23:12 Ava Chen: Proposal: Redwood covers first-line triage for the first 2 hours post-shift, then rotate to Nimble oncall. We'll include escalation paths in the runbook.
00:23:23 Leah Solomon: That works as long as we have oncall contacts in the doc and a Slack channel integration.

00:27:10 Omar Ruiz: Can we talk about cost controls — our finance team expects a hard cap for the pilot phase. How do we ensure we don't blow past it?
00:27:18 Marcus Lee: We can set per-route budget limits and hard token caps. The platform supports throttling when you hit X tokens per minute. We'll configure a conservative cap for the canary and notify when you hit 70% of budget.
00:27:33 Dana Ortiz: Good. Also can you map expected token burn per 1k interactions so finance can model it?
00:27:40 Ava Chen: I'll include a per-1k projection in the pricing doc — with low, mid, high scenarios.

00:31:05 Marcus Lee: Small gotcha from infra: when you use Private with HSM, cold starts are longer on node boot because of key unwrap. In the pilot we observed an additional ~30-50ms per first few requests per node. If startup time matters, we recommend keeping a small warm pool.
00:31:22 Benji Park: We'll consider warm pool. Is that charged as reserved capacity?
00:31:26 Marcus Lee: In Dedicated/Private you can reserve a standing pool; we'll denote it in pricing as warm capacity.

00:34:00 Ava Chen: Ok let's lock the canary cadence: Day 0 (cutover start) 5% for 6 hours with 100% tracing, Day 1 increase to 25% if gates pass, Day 2 -> 50%, Day 3 full shift. Gate checks at each step: p99 < 350ms, errors < 0.2%, cost within 120% of projection.
00:34:22 Leah Solomon: I want explicit thresholds for synthetic probes too — e.g., run 1 probe every 10s for the top 50 traffic flows. If any probe exceeds threshold, trigger immediate rollback.
00:34:35 Marcus Lee: We'll bake that into the monitor rules.

00:36:10 Dana Ortiz: For compliance signoffs: we need SOC/ISO docs and the KMS/HSM design by 6/16. Can Redwood provide the security pack and the network ACLs?
00:36:21 Marcus Lee: Yes, security pack and network egress rules will be delivered. We'll include a checklist for your auditors.

00:38:50 Ava Chen: Final risk list: 1) Cold-KV warmup causing p99 spikes 2) BI job burst colliding with user traffic 3) KMS egress policy not cleared by security by 6/16 4) Unexpected cost overrun. Mitigations noted.
00:39:15 Omar Ruiz: For the BI job we will implement queuing on the application side first to reduce burst.
00:39:25 Benji Park: We'll also add ingress rate-limiter for batch jobs.

00:41:40 Ava Chen: Any other blockers?
00:41:44 Dana Ortiz: Our security lead wants an explicit data flow diagram showing what leaves our VPC — can you include that in the runbook doc?
00:41:52 Marcus Lee: Yes, will include a redacted data flow and the FQDNs/IPs for KMS calls.
00:41:56 Leah Solomon: Also include oncall phone numbers and SLAs for rollback.

00:44:10 Ava Chen: OK recap of next steps: I'll send pricing and the canary traffic matrix; Marcus will post the tuning artifacts and the batching config; Nimble will run the heavy soak and confirm security gating. Decision meeting scheduled 2026-06-18 15:00 UTC for go/no-go.
00:44:28 Dana Ortiz: Sounds good.
00:44:33 Omar Ruiz: One more request — can we have a short tabletop run-through 24 hours before cutover?
00:44:40 Ava Chen: Absolutely, we'll schedule a 30-minute runbook dry run on 2026-06-17.
00:44:50 Priya Nair: I'll coordinate and send invites.
00:45:00 Marcus Lee: I'll also post a short checklist for the dry run.
00:45:10 Ava Chen: Great. Thanks everyone, we'll follow up via email and Slack.
00:45:18 Dana Ortiz: Thanks.

AUTO ACTION ITEMS (automatically detected, may be noisy):
- Post-run tuning config Marcus Lee https://redwood.app/pilot/configs/sprint5
- Pricing and cost projection Ava Chen (attached)
- Security pack and KMS egress doc Marcus Lee
- Oncall rotation and paging Leah to confirm

End of transcript.

topics: Benchmarks and latency vs quality tradeoffs

Priya Natarajan: Prepare kernel mapping runbook and suggested quantization profiles by 2026-05-07
Jin Park: Upload representative prompt set and target latency envelope by 2026-05-01

source.transcription:
Meeting header: 2026-04-28 15:00 UTC, duration ~43m. Attendees: Alex (Redwood AE), Priya (Redwood SE), Maya (NimbleHealth CTO), Tom (Nimble SRE), Jin (Nimble ML)

[00:00] Alex: Hey everybody, thanks for joining. Quick agenda — we want to run through our model catalog, show how the benchmark sheet maps to pick candidates, and then talk about deployment compatibility (hosted/dedicated/ private). I know you sent over the summarization prompts, thanks Jin.

[00:22] Maya: Yep, thanks. high level — our main constraint is P95 latency under 600ms for single-turn summaries, and of course PHI never leaves our VPC. so we're trying to find models that balance cost and accuracy.

[00:35] Priya: Got it. I'll pull up the catalog. A couple of notes before we start: our catalog has performance profiles — tokens/sec, avg latencies at different seq lengths, and quantified quality metrics from our eval suite. we also include a compatibility matrix for hardware kernels, and suggested quantization profiles.

[00:55] Tom: Quick security Q up front: we must run in our VPC or a private deployment, and KMS integration is required. does Redwood's private option support customer-managed KMS and HSM? and audit logs at token level?

[01:12] Alex: Short answer yes — private supports customer KMS through our integration and you get audit logs. I'll send the SOC2/ISO pack after the call. note that hosted can be used if you want a faster pilot with encrypted-in-transit and at-rest, but for PHI we normally recommend private. we'll share the compliance slide deck.

[01:34] Maya: OK good. Jin, can you outline the prompt shape and how many tokens per request on average?

[01:42] Jin: Sure — average input ~420 tokens, outputs ~120 tokens, so 540 tokens total. concurrency estimate initial is ~10 RPS but bursty during business hours. cost is a big factor for us, so if quantization and batching help keep unit cost down that'd be huge.

[02:03] Priya: Right. So on the catalog side, filters you care about: model family size (7B vs 13B), quantization friendliness, kernel maturity for A100 vs H100, and validated latency at your seq lengths. in the UI i can toggle those and get a shortlist.

[02:26] Alex: We're going to demo a quick pick flow: pick metrics -> run bench -> map kernels.

[02:33] Priya: (screen) Okay. Here is the 'compatibility matrix' view — rows are models, columns are infra targets and quant profiles. green means validated kernel and expected latency within our target for the measured batch/seq. yellow means compatible but needs a custom kernel path. sometimes labels are off — e.g., we annotate K-V cache as "KV cash" in the auto-transcript, so don't be thrown off if you see odd names in exported logs.

[03:05] Maya: ha, nice. can you show an example candidate for our constraints — say a 13B that's been quantized and still meets quality?

[03:12] Priya: Sure. here's ModelX-13B-q4 (note: shorthand). Bench results: for 540 token runs at concurrency 10, we see median 420ms on H100 with quantization q4-pt, P95 around 560ms. Quality drop vs fp16 is ~1.2% on our clinical summarization eval (we'll show the eval set later). cost per 1k tokens comes down roughly 40% vs fp16.

[03:47] Tom: That P95 is promising. how consistent is that across longer context — we do occasionally have 1.2k token inputs.

[03:55] Priya: Bench sheet includes a sequence-length view — for 1.2k tokens the P95 increases to ~900ms on same infra; we'd either pick a smaller model or route longer contexts to a higher-capacity variant. That's where routing policies come in — you can set prefix routing to route >1k to ModelX-large and <=1k to ModelX-13B-q4.

[04:20] Jin: ok so that's the model variant routing, right? does Redwood's runtime support automated fallback if the dedicated pool is saturated?

[04:28] Alex: Yes — smart routing can fallback to hosted compatible variants or to a cheaper quantized variant within your dedicated pool, depending on your policy. fallback still respects data handling rules if you're on private; we don't route PHI out of your VPC unless you enable that.

[04:46] Maya: Good. about kernels — you mentioned the kernel maturity column. we run on A100s in prod. do you have H100-only optimizations that won't run on our A100s?

[04:57] Priya: We try to map kernels to both A100 and H100. some new attention kernels are H100-optimized and give 15-25% perf uplift; if they're not supported on A100 we mark the matrix as yellow and recommend an alternate kernel or small patch. we also include a note if a kernel is queued for upstream merge.

[05:18] Tom: Suppose we commit to Dedicated — what's the typical SLA and autoscale behavior? our peak traffic is 40 RPS during syncs.

[05:26] Alex: Dedicated gives you reserved capacity and autoscale rules you can configure — you can set burst pools for short windows. SLA is in the enterprise docs but typically 99.9% with latency SLO targets depending on commit tier. i'll attach the dedicated spec.

[05:41] Maya: On the bench spreadsheet — will you include raw outputs so we can run our own eval scripts? we want to run our clinical scoring.

[05:49] Priya: Yes, we can give you raw outputs and the eval harness. for the pilot we'll include the bench CSV with per-run latencies, seed, prompt id, and a link to a GCS bucket with outputs. note: sometimes model names in the CSV are shortened; we add a column for canonical model id.

[06:10] Jin: Perfect. for the pilot scope, i'd like us to try two model families: one 13B quantized candidate and one 7B quantized cheap path, to see cost vs quality.

[06:22] Alex: Sounds reasonable. Our recommended success criteria: maintain clinical F1 within 2% of your current best, P95 <= 600ms for typical 540 token runs, and cost per 1k tokens below your target (we can parameterize that in the spreadsheet).

[06:40] Maya: timeline question — how quickly can we get a pilot running if we sign up for dedicated vs private?

[06:48] Alex: Hosted pilot is fastest — we can get you onboarded in a couple days. For Dedicated it's usually 2-3 weeks for capacity and contracts; Private (VPC/on-prem) can be 4-6 weeks depending on networking. we'll put expected timelines in the proposal.

[07:05] Tom: Regarding networking: do you support private peering and specific CIDR ranges? and can we enforce egress rules so nothing leaves our subnet?

[07:14] Priya: Yes, VPC peering and private connectivity are supported. and for private deployments you control egress; we can also do air-gapped installs for on-prem if needed.

source.transcript:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-02-11
Start: 15:30 UTC
Duration: ~58 minutes
Location: Zoom
Recording: yes

Attendees: Alex (Redwood AE), Jordan (Redwood SE), Samira (Product), Ethan (SRE) — Priya (NimbleHealth ML), Marcus (Infra), Lena (Head AI)

Auto-summary (auto gen, may be rough):
- Deep dive on quantization options (INT8 / FP8 / GPTQ / AWQ) and how they map to accuracy/latency tradeoffs for NimbleHealth clinical prompts.
- Discussed KV cache interactions, batching, and on-prem VPC constraints.
- Agreed on next steps: share micro-benchmarks, customer to send prompt corpus, schedule repro session.

Topics: quantization accuracy regressions, calibration data, batch sizing, prefix caching, VPC KMS rules, fallback strategies.

Transcript body:
[00:00:05] Alex (Redwood AE): Hey everyone, thanks for joining — short agenda: Jordan will run through micro-benchmarks and tradeoffs, then we'll talk about your on-prem constraints and next steps.
[00:00:16] Priya (NimbleHealth): Thanks, Alex. We mainly care about latency at p95 for clinical note generation and zero-tolerance for hallucinations on protected fields. We're trying to see if quant can save costs without regression.
[00:00:28] Jordan (Redwood SE): Yep that's the right framing. Quick callout — quantization isn't magic; it's a set of tradeoffs. INT8 gives big memory savings and often good perf, but you do see accuracy drift on sensitive tokens unless calibration is carefully done. GPTQ/AWQ are post-training quant methods more optimized for LLMs.
[00:00:44] Marcus (Infra): Quick Q — do we need FP8 for best perf on A100 vs T4? Our on-prem fleet is mixed.
[00:00:50] Jordan (SE): Short answer: FP8 can get you single-step speedups on certain hardware that has FP8 fast paths, but FP8 benefits depend on kernel support. For A100 gen3 maybe, but older T4s won't help. INT8 + AWQ/GPTQ often gives the best cross-hardware portability.
[00:01:05] Lena (Head AI): We ran an internal run with GPTQ and saw a small BLEU drop on our summarization set; some outputs were shorter. Any suggestions?
[00:01:15] Jordan (SE): That’s expected. GPTQ can change distribution subtly. We typically do three things: 1) calibrate on domain prompts (you sent a few examples earlier, which is great), 2) apply per-channel quantization where available, and 3) tune decoding temperature/penalty to match length. Also consider hybrid approach — keep key layers in FP16 or FP32.
[00:01:36] Priya: On the calibration point — how many samples do you need? We can't share PHI, so we'll scrub.
[00:01:42] Samira (Product): For useful calibration you generally want a few thousand tokens across prompt/response pairs; but even 200-500 targeted examples can catch big regressions. The team often starts with 500-1000 domain-specific samples.
[00:01:56] Priya: OK we'll aim to provide 1k scrubbed prompts.
[00:02:02] Jordan (SE): Great. Now about AWQ specifically — AWQ is optimized for transformer weight quant with attention-aware heuristics; in practice we've seen AWQ hold accuracy better than naive INT8 on many decoder-only models, but it's also more sensitive to outlier dimensions and can require a slightly different calibration flow.
[00:02:18] Marcus: Are there known pathological cases where AWQ makes things worse?
[00:02:22] Jordan: Rarely but yes — when models have heavy low-rank adapters or very sparse heads, AWQ's heuristics can mis-estimate scaling for some heads, leading to token-level regressions. That's why we recommend a quick regression test suite before promoting a quantized build.
[00:02:36] [00:02:36] (audio noise) Speaker 4: mm-hmm
[00:02:42] Priya: Can you walk through an example of a regression we should look for?
[00:02:46] Jordan: Sure — example: protected PHI fields being replaced or redacted incorrectly, or repetition/early-stopping for clinical bullet lists. On one customer case, GPTQ caused truncation in long templates because length-prediction tokens shifted slightly. The fix was hybridizing the last 4 transformer blocks to FP16.
[00:03:06] Alex: Another operational approach is to add targeted evals that check protected-field fidelity. Redwood's Optimize product can instrument prompt sets and watch for drift post-quant.
[00:03:15] Lena: Tell us about KV cache implications — we rely heavily on prefix caching to speed up common templates.
[00:03:20] Jordan: KV cache itself is orthogonal to weight quantization, but quant affects memory layout and KV cache eviction policies indirectly. For example, INT8 weights reduce model memory leaving more headroom for longer KV caches, which helps latency for repeated contexts. But watch out for alignment/padding — some quant kernels require different memory alignment that can actually increase per-token latency in tight loops if not tuned.
[00:03:45] Marcus: So batching — our throughput target is 200 rps during peak, p95 latency under 350ms. If we apply quant, can we meet that on our 4xA100 nodes?
[00:03:55] Jordan: With proper batching and KV cache warm-up, yes. Our rough numbers: FP16 baseline on 4xA100 for your model family gave ~150 rps with p95 ~420ms; INT8+AWQ tuning pushed that to ~230 rps p95 ~320ms. But your mileage will vary; that's why we want to run micro-benchmarks on your exact prompts.
[00:04:15] Priya: You mentioned micro-benchmarks earlier — what metrics should we capture?
[00:04:19] Jordan: Key metrics: p50/p95/p99 latency, tokens/sec throughput, memory used (model+kvcache), stall events (garbage collection/IO waits), and accuracy deltas on the prompt suite (exact-match, containment for PHI, semantic similarity). Also track tail latencies during batch packing.
[00:04:36] [00:04:36] Alex: We'll provide a perf script that logs these, plus a small self-serve dashboard.
[00:04:41] Marcus: For VPC/on-prem testing — we have strict egress rules and a locked-down KMS. How do we get Redwood to run a repro without giving keys?
[00:04:49] Ethan (SRE): For private deployments we can use a short-lived test token and have you run the perf scripts inside your VPC against a Redwood-agent image. Alternatively, we can provide a reproducible docker bundle that runs fully offline; the only requirement is we need a list of allowed ports and KMS handshake endpoints if you want us to validate encryption at rest.
[00:05:06] Priya: We prefer the docker bundle.
[00:05:08] Jordan: Okay — AWQ/GPTQ repro docker includes quant toolchain, a small sample model shard, and a harness to run your prompt suite. Note: we don't include customer data; you'll run the harness inside your network and then share the metrics artifact.
[00:05:21] Priya: Perfect.
[00:05:24] Lena: Are there licensing or export issues with GPTQ or AWQ implementations? We have legal constraints.
[00:05:30] Alex: Good point. AWQ toolchain is under a permissive license in our distro, GPTQ forks vary. We'll include a license summary in the repo we share. Also, Redwood's Private package supports air-gapped deployment under enterprise contract if needed.
[00:05:45] Marcus: Something we hit last time — after quant we had a few memory alignment faults in our TF serving stack because kernels expected 64-byte alignment. Will Redwood's runtime handle alignment automatically?
[00:05:55] Jordan: Our runtime includes kernel selection and memory padding logic but when using in-house kernel variants in on-prem builds we sometimes need to set environment flags (REDWOOD_ALIGN=64) or tune allocator. We'll document the flags in the runbook.
[00:06:09] [00:06:09] (brief crosstalk) Speaker 4: right right
[00:06:15] Priya: On accuracy we can't accept more than 0.5% drop on our key metrics — is that realistic with INT8?
[00:06:22] Jordan: With domain calibration and hybrid FP16 on sensitive layers, yes it's realistic for many models, but not guaranteed. Some models, particularly small fine-tuned instruction-tuned models, are more brittle. We'll measure with your scrubbed prompts and then recommend either GPTQ/AWQ or hybridization.
[00:06:39] Alex: Another operational guard is rollout policy — canary quant builds at low percent traffic, roll back automatically on regression signals. Redwood's Console supports canary + automatic fallback to FP16 model in less than a minute if an eval alert hits.
[00:06:54] Marcus: How invasive is hybridizing the last N layers? Do we need to retrain?
[00:06:59] Jordan: No retraining — hybrid means keep weight tensors for select layers in FP16/FP32 while quantizing others. That increases model size slightly but preserves critical distribution info. The tradeoff is per-token latency might go up a bit for those layers but often still net positive.
[00:07:14] Priya: On the topic of AWQ vs GPTQ — which is easier to debug when you see regressions?
[00:07:20] Jordan: GPTQ tends to be more deterministic in behaviour because it's a quantization algorithm tied closely to the per-channel statistics; AWQ adds heuristic attention-aware steps, which can make debugging trickier. But AWQ often gives better out-of-the-box accuracy.
[00:07:33] Lena: Any guidance on decoding hyperparams to counteract short outputs after quant?
[00:07:37] Jordan: Increase repetition penalty slightly, bump min-length, or adjust length-penalty and temperature. We usually snapshot decoder hyperparams as part of the quant rollout.
[00:07:46] [00:07:46] (typing sounds)
[00:07:49] Alex: Quick pragmatic checklist for NimbleHealth: 1) Provide 1k scrubbed prompts; 2) Redwood shares AWQ/GPTQ docker + perf script; 3) Nimble runs inside VPC and returns metrics + sample outputs; 4) joint call to review diffs and pick quant strategy; 5) schedule canary rollout.
[00:08:08] Priya: Sounds good.
[00:08:11] Marcus: A question on monitoring — if quantization causes a silent semantic drift, what signals should we set to catch it before customers see it?
[00:08:19] Samira (Product): We recommend a layered approach: 1) unit-level exact-match tests for templates, 2) semantic similarity scoring against golden responses (embedding drift), 3) token-level anomaly detectors (unexpected token distributions), and 4) user-facing quality metrics (NPS or error reports). Redwood's Optimize can automate the first three.
[00:08:38] Priya: For semantic drift thresholds, any rule-of-thumb?
[00:08:41] Jordan: Watch for >2% drop in semantic similarity or >0.5% drop in exact-match for critical templates. But thresholds depend on your risk tolerance — for PHI you want near-zero tolerance.
[00:08:52] [00:08:52] (brief pause)
[00:08:57] Marcus: Two operational concerns: disk I/O during quant conversion (we saw CPU spikes) and kernel compile times for on-prem. Any mitigation?
[00:09:04] Jordan: Convert jobs are CPU-heavy and I/O bound; run them off-peak and use local SSDs to speed up sharding. For kernel compiles, prebuilt kernel images can be supplied — Redwood will provide these for your target kernels to avoid long compile cycles.
[00:09:18] Lena: If we see a regression in the canary, what's the rollback runbook?
[00:09:22] Alex: Automated rollback via Console — canary traffic is small, and if the eval alert triggers, we immediately shift traffic to the FP16 variant and capture failing traces. Post-mortem includes differential token analysis and layer-level ablation tests.
[00:09:36] Priya: We need to make sure logs don't leak PHI in traces.
[00:09:40] Ethan (SRE): We'll redact traces and only capture hashes/embeddings for drift checks; full outputs stay inside your VPC unless you opt-in.
[00:09:48] [00:09:48] (keyboard clack)
[00:09:52] Alex: Ok wrapping with timings — we'll share the docker and perf script by 2025-02-13, Jordan will drop the runbook, Marcus will confirm infra readiness by 2025-02-18, and we'll reconvene after metric upload.
[00:10:05] Priya: Thanks — one last nit: are there specific tokenizer edge cases with quant? We had mis-tokenization in previous runs.
[00:10:12] Jordan: Tokenization is upstream and doesn't change with quantization, but if quantization leads to changed whitespace or special token handling in generated text, that could surface. We recommend freezing tokenizer versions and including a tokenization sanity check in the regression suite.
[00:10:27] Alex: Great. Anything else?
[00:10:30] Lena: No, thanks team.
[00:10:33] Alex: Thanks everyone — action items as noted. We'll follow up with the shared folder link.

[rough end-of-transcript notes]:
- Transcript auto-generated; some names or acronyms may be mis-transcribed (e.g., "K V cash" for KV cache).
- Syllables/filler included; occasional overlapping speech labeled generically.
- Links shared during call: https://redwood.example.com/awq-docker (shared repo placeholder), https://redwood.example.com/perf-script

End of meeting.

text:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-02-11
Start: 15:30 UTC
Duration: ~58 minutes
Location: Zoom
Recording: yes

Attendees: Alex (Redwood AE), Jordan (Redwood SE), Samira (Product), Ethan (SRE) — Priya (NimbleHealth ML), Marcus (Infra), Lena (Head AI)

Auto-summary (auto gen, may be rough):
- Deep dive on quantization options (INT8 / FP8 / GPTQ / AWQ) and how they map to accuracy/latency tradeoffs for NimbleHealth clinical prompts.
- Discussed KV cache interactions, batching, and on-prem VPC constraints.
- Agreed on next steps: share micro-benchmarks, customer to send prompt corpus, schedule repro session.

Topics: quantization accuracy regressions, calibration data, batch sizing, prefix caching, VPC KMS rules, fallback strategies.

Transcript body:
[00:00:05] Alex (Redwood AE): Hey everyone, thanks for joining — short agenda: Jordan will run through micro-benchmarks and tradeoffs, then we'll talk about your on-prem constraints and next steps.
[00:00:16] Priya (NimbleHealth): Thanks, Alex. We mainly care about latency at p95 for clinical note generation and zero-tolerance for hallucinations on protected fields. We're trying to see if quant can save costs without regression.
[00:00:28] Jordan (Redwood SE): Yep that's the right framing. Quick callout — quantization isn't magic; it's a set of tradeoffs. INT8 gives big memory savings and often good perf, but you do see accuracy drift on sensitive tokens unless calibration is carefully done. GPTQ/AWQ are post-training quant methods more optimized for LLMs.
[00:00:44] Marcus (Infra): Quick Q — do we need FP8 for best perf on A100 vs T4? Our on-prem fleet is mixed.
[00:00:50] Jordan (SE): Short answer: FP8 can get you single-step speedups on certain hardware that has FP8 fast paths, but FP8 benefits depend on kernel support. For A100 gen3 maybe, but older T4s won't help. INT8 + AWQ/GPTQ often gives the best cross-hardware portability.
[00:01:05] Lena (Head AI): We ran an internal run with GPTQ and saw a small BLEU drop on our summarization set; some outputs were shorter. Any suggestions?
[00:01:15] Jordan (SE): That’s expected. GPTQ can change distribution subtly. We typically do three things: 1) calibrate on domain prompts (you sent a few examples earlier, which is great), 2) apply per-channel quantization where available, and 3) tune decoding temperature/penalty to match length. Also consider hybrid approach — keep key layers in FP16 or FP32.
[00:01:36] Priya: On the calibration point — how many samples do you need? We can't share PHI, so we'll scrub.
[00:01:42] Samira (Product): For useful calibration you generally want a few thousand tokens across prompt/response pairs; but even 200-500 targeted examples can catch big regressions. The team often starts with 500-1000 domain-specific samples.
[00:01:56] Priya: OK we'll aim to provide 1k scrubbed prompts.
[00:02:02] Jordan (SE): Great. Now about AWQ specifically — AWQ is optimized for transformer weight quant with attention-aware heuristics; in practice we've seen AWQ hold accuracy better than naive INT8 on many decoder-only models, but it's also more sensitive to outlier dimensions and can require a slightly different calibration flow.
[00:02:18] Marcus: Are there known pathological cases where AWQ makes things worse?
[00:02:22] Jordan: Rarely but yes — when models have heavy low-rank adapters or very sparse heads, AWQ's heuristics can mis-estimate scaling for some heads, leading to token-level regressions. That's why we recommend a quick regression test suite before promoting a quantized build.
[00:02:36] [00:02:36] (audio noise) Speaker 4: mm-hmm
[00:02:42] Priya: Can you walk through an example of a regression we should look for?
[00:02:46] Jordan: Sure — example: protected PHI fields being replaced or redacted incorrectly, or repetition/early-stopping for clinical bullet lists. On one customer case, GPTQ caused truncation in long templates because length-prediction tokens shifted slightly. The fix was hybridizing the last 4 transformer blocks to FP16.
[00:03:06] Alex: Another operational approach is to add targeted evals that check protected-field fidelity. Redwood's Optimize product can instrument prompt sets and watch for drift post-quant.
[00:03:15] Lena: Tell us about KV cache implications — we rely heavily on prefix caching to speed up common templates.
[00:03:20] Jordan: KV cache itself is orthogonal to weight quantization, but quant affects memory layout and KV cache eviction policies indirectly. For example, INT8 weights reduce model memory leaving more headroom for longer KV caches, which helps latency for repeated contexts. But watch out for alignment/padding — some quant kernels require different memory alignment that can actually increase per-token latency in tight loops if not tuned.
[00:03:45] Marcus: So batching — our throughput target is 200 rps during peak, p95 latency under 350ms. If we apply quant, can we meet that on our 4xA100 nodes?
[00:03:55] Jordan: With proper batching and KV cache warm-up, yes. Our rough numbers: FP16 baseline on 4xA100 for your model family gave ~150 rps with p95 ~420ms; INT8+AWQ tuning pushed that to ~230 rps p95 ~320ms. But your mileage will vary; that's why we want to run micro-benchmarks on your exact prompts.
[00:04:15] Priya: You mentioned micro-benchmarks earlier — what metrics should we capture?
[00:04:19] Jordan: Key metrics: p50/p95/p99 latency, tokens/sec throughput, memory used (model+kvcache), stall events (garbage collection/IO waits), and accuracy deltas on the prompt suite (exact-match, containment for PHI, semantic similarity). Also track tail latencies during batch packing.
[00:04:36] [00:04:36] Alex: We'll provide a perf script that logs these, plus a small self-serve dashboard.
[00:04:41] Marcus: For VPC/on-prem testing — we have strict egress rules and a locked-down KMS. How do we get Redwood to run a repro without giving keys?
[00:04:49] Ethan (SRE): For private deployments we can use a short-lived test token and have you run the perf scripts inside your VPC against a Redwood-agent image. Alternatively, we can provide a reproducible docker bundle that runs fully offline; the only requirement is we need a list of allowed ports and KMS handshake endpoints if you want us to validate encryption at rest.
[00:05:06] Priya: We prefer the docker bundle.
[00:05:08] Jordan: Okay — AWQ/GPTQ repro docker includes quant toolchain, a small sample model shard, and a harness to run your prompt suite. Note: we don't include customer data; you'll run the harness inside your network and then share the metrics artifact.
[00:05:21] Priya: Perfect.
[00:05:24] Lena: Are there licensing or export issues with GPTQ or AWQ implementations? We have legal constraints.
[00:05:30] Alex: Good point. AWQ toolchain is under a permissive license in our distro, GPTQ forks vary. We'll include a license summary in the repo we share. Also, Redwood's Private package supports air-gapped deployment under enterprise contract if needed.
[00:05:45] Marcus: Something we hit last time — after quant we had a few memory alignment faults in our TF serving stack because kernels expected 64-byte alignment. Will Redwood's runtime handle alignment automatically?
[00:05:55] Jordan: Our runtime includes kernel selection and memory padding logic but when using in-house kernel variants in on-prem builds we sometimes need to set environment flags (REDWOOD_ALIGN=64) or tune allocator. We'll document the flags in the runbook.
[00:06:09] [00:06:09] (brief crosstalk) Speaker 4: right right
[00:06:15] Priya: On accuracy we can't accept more than 0.5% drop on our key metrics — is that realistic with INT8?
[00:06:22] Jordan: With domain calibration and hybrid FP16 on sensitive layers, yes it's realistic for many models, but not guaranteed. Some models, particularly small fine-tuned instruction-tuned models, are more brittle. We'll measure with your scrubbed prompts and then recommend either GPTQ/AWQ or hybridization.
[00:06:39] Alex: Another operational guard is rollout policy — canary quant builds at low percent traffic, roll back automatically on regression signals. Redwood's Console supports canary + automatic fallback to FP16 model in less than a minute if an eval alert hits.
[00:06:54] Marcus: How invasive is hybridizing the last N layers? Do we need to retrain?
[00:06:59] Jordan: No retraining — hybrid means keep weight tensors for select layers in FP16/FP32 while quantizing others. That increases model size slightly but preserves critical distribution info. The tradeoff is per-token latency might go up a bit for those layers but often still net positive.
[00:07:14] Priya: On the topic of AWQ vs GPTQ — which is easier to debug when you see regressions?
[00:07:20] Jordan: GPTQ tends to be more deterministic in behaviour because it's a quantization algorithm tied closely to the per-channel statistics; AWQ adds heuristic attention-aware steps, which can make debugging trickier. But AWQ often gives better out-of-the-box accuracy.
[00:07:33] Lena: Any guidance on decoding hyperparams to counteract short outputs after quant?
[00:07:37] Jordan: Increase repetition penalty slightly, bump min-length, or adjust length-penalty and temperature. We usually snapshot decoder hyperparams as part of the quant rollout.
[00:07:46] [00:07:46] (typing sounds)
[00:07:49] Alex: Quick pragmatic checklist for NimbleHealth: 1) Provide 1k scrubbed prompts; 2) Redwood shares AWQ/GPTQ docker + perf script; 3) Nimble runs inside VPC and returns metrics + sample outputs; 4) joint call to review diffs and pick quant strategy; 5) schedule canary rollout.
[00:08:08] Priya: Sounds good.
[00:08:11] Marcus: A question on monitoring — if quantization causes a silent semantic drift, what signals should we set to catch it before customers see it?
[00:08:19] Samira (Product): We recommend a layered approach: 1) unit-level exact-match tests for templates, 2) semantic similarity scoring against golden responses (embedding drift), 3) token-level anomaly detectors (unexpected token distributions), and 4) user-facing quality metrics (NPS or error reports). Redwood's Optimize can automate the first three.
[00:08:38] Priya: For semantic drift thresholds, any rule-of-thumb?
[00:08:41] Jordan: Watch for >2% drop in semantic similarity or >0.5% drop in exact-match for critical templates. But thresholds depend on your risk tolerance — for PHI you want near-zero tolerance.
[00:08:52] [00:08:52] (brief pause)
[00:08:57] Marcus: Two operational concerns: disk I/O during quant conversion (we saw CPU spikes) and kernel compile times for on-prem. Any mitigation?
[00:09:04] Jordan: Convert jobs are CPU-heavy and I/O bound; run them off-peak and use local SSDs to speed up sharding. For kernel compiles, prebuilt kernel images can be supplied — Redwood will provide these for your target kernels to avoid long compile cycles.
[00:09:18] Lena: If we see a regression in the canary, what's the rollback runbook?
[00:09:22] Alex: Automated rollback via Console — canary traffic is small, and if the eval alert triggers, we immediately shift traffic to the FP16 variant and capture failing traces. Post-mortem includes differential token analysis and layer-level ablation tests.
[00:09:36] Priya: We need to make sure logs don't leak PHI in traces.
[00:09:40] Ethan (SRE): We'll redact traces and only capture hashes/embeddings for drift checks; full outputs stay inside your VPC unless you opt-in.
[00:09:48] [00:09:48] (keyboard clack)
[00:09:52] Alex: Ok wrapping with timings — we'll share the docker and perf script by 2025-02-13, Jordan will drop the runbook, Marcus will confirm infra readiness by 2025-02-18, and we'll reconvene after metric upload.
[00:10:05] Priya: Thanks — one last nit: are there specific tokenizer edge cases with quant? We had mis-tokenization in previous runs.
[00:10:12] Jordan: Tokenization is upstream and doesn't change with quantization, but if quantization leads to changed whitespace or special token handling in generated text, that could surface. We recommend freezing tokenizer versions and including a tokenization sanity check in the regression suite.
[00:10:27] Alex: Great. Anything else?
[00:10:30] Lena: No, thanks team.
[00:10:33] Alex: Thanks everyone — action items as noted. We'll follow up with the shared folder link.
